documentation: Convenience function to parse array from a signal.

functions.name: hyperspy.drawing.utils.plot_images
documentation:
Plot multiple images either as sub-images or overlayed in one figure.

# Parameters

images

: `images` should be a list of Signals to plot. For
  {class}`~.api.signals.BaseSignal` with navigation dimensions 2 and
  signal dimension 0, the signal will be transposed to form a `Signal2D`.
  Multi-dimensional images will have each plane plotted as a separate
  image. If any of the signal shapes is not suitable, a ValueError will be
  raised.

cmap

: The colormap used for the images, by default uses the setting
  `color map signal` from the plot preferences. A list of colormaps can
  also be provided, and the images will cycle through them. Optionally,
  the value `'mpl_colors'` will cause the cmap to loop through the
  default `matplotlib` colors (to match with the default output of the
  {func}`~.api.plot.plot_spectra` method).
  Note: if using more than one colormap, using the `'single'`
  option for `colorbar` is disallowed.

no_nans

: If True, set nans to zero for plotting.

per_row

: The number of plots in each row.

label

: Control the title labeling of the plotted images.
  If None, no titles will be shown.
  If 'auto' (default), function will try to determine suitable titles
  using Signal2D titles, falling back to the 'titles' option if no good
  short titles are detected.
  Works best if all images to be plotted have the same beginning
  to their titles.
  If 'titles', the title from each image's `metadata.General.title`
  will be used.
  If any other single str, images will be labeled in sequence using
  that str as a prefix.
  If a list of str, the list elements will be used to determine the
  labels (repeated, if necessary).

labelwrap

: Integer specifying the number of characters that will be used on
  one line.
  If the function returns an unexpected blank figure, lower this
  value to reduce overlap of the labels between figures.

suptitle

: Title to use at the top of the figure. If called with label='auto',
  this parameter will override the automatically determined title.

suptitle_fontsize

: Font size to use for super title at top of figure.

colorbar

: Controls the type of colorbars that are plotted, incompatible with
  `overlay=True`.
  If 'default', same as 'multi' when `overlay=False`, otherwise same
  as `None`.
  If 'multi', individual colorbars are plotted for each (non-RGB) image.
  If 'single', all (non-RGB) images are plotted on the same scale,
  and one colorbar is shown for all.
  If None, no colorbar is plotted.

centre_colormap

: If True, the centre of the color scheme is set to zero. This is
  particularly useful when using diverging color schemes. If 'auto'
  (default), diverging color schemes are automatically centred.

scalebar

: If None (or False), no scalebars will be added to the images.
  If 'all', scalebars will be added to all images.
  If list of ints, scalebars will be added to each image specified.

scalebar_color

: A valid MPL color string; will be used as the scalebar color.

axes_decor

: Controls how the axes are displayed on each image; default is 'all'.
  If 'all', both ticks and axis labels will be shown.
  If 'ticks', no axis labels will be shown, but ticks/labels will.
  If 'off', all decorations and frame will be disabled.
  If None, no axis decorations will be shown, but ticks/frame will.

padding

: This parameter controls the spacing between images.
  If None, default options will be used.
  Otherwise, supply a dictionary with the spacing options as
  keywords and desired values as values.
  Values should be supplied as used in
  {func}`matplotlib.pyplot.subplots_adjust`,
  and can be 'left', 'bottom', 'right', 'top', 'wspace' (width) and
  'hspace' (height).

tight_layout

: If true, hyperspy will attempt to improve image placement in
  figure using matplotlib's tight_layout.
  If false, repositioning images inside the figure will be left as
  an exercise for the user.

aspect

: If 'auto', aspect ratio is auto determined, subject to min_asp.
  If 'square', image will be forced onto square display.
  If 'equal', aspect ratio of 1 will be enforced.
  If float (or int/long), given value will be used.

min_asp

: Minimum aspect ratio to be used when plotting images.

namefrac_thresh

: Threshold to use for auto-labeling. This parameter controls how
  much of the titles must be the same for the auto-shortening of
  labels to activate. Can vary from 0 to 1. Smaller values
  encourage shortening of titles by auto-labeling, while larger
  values will require more overlap in titles before activing the
  auto-label code.

fig

: If set, the images will be plotted to an existing matplotlib figure.
  If the parameter `ax` is provided, this parameter will be ignored
  and the figure will be obtained from the `ax` parameter.

ax

: The matplotlib axes to use to display the images.
  When using `overlay=True`, `ax` must be a matplotlib axis.
  If None, new matplotlib axes will be created as required.

vmin, vmax: scalar, str, None

: If str, formatted as 'xth', use this value to calculate the percentage
  of pixels that are left out of the lower and upper bounds.
  For example, for a vmin of '1th', 1% of the lowest will be ignored to
  estimate the minimum value. Similarly, for a vmax value of '1th', 1%
  of the highest value will be ignored in the estimation of the maximum
  value. It must be in the range [0, 100].
  See {func}`numpy.percentile` for more explanation.
  If None, use the percentiles value set in the preferences.
  If float or integer, keep this value as bounds.
  Note: vmin is ignored when overlaying images.

overlay

: If True, overlays the images with different colors rather than plotting
  each image as a subplot.

colors

: If list, it must contains colors acceptable to matplotlib [^footnote-1].
  If `'auto'`, colors will be taken from matplotlib.colors.BASE_COLORS.

alphas

: Float value or a list of floats corresponding to the alpha value of
  each color.

legend_picking: bool, optional

: If True (default), an image can be toggled on and off by clicking on
  the legended line. For `overlay=True` only.

legend_loc

: This parameter controls where the legend is placed on the figure
  see the {func}`matplotlib.pyplot.legend` docstring for valid values

pixel_size_factor

: If `None` (default), the size of the figure is taken from the
  matplotlib `rcParams`. Otherwise sets the size of the figure when
  plotting an overlay image. The higher the number the larger the figure
  and therefore a greater number of pixels are used. This value will be
  ignored if a Figure is provided.

kwargs, optional

: Additional keyword arguments passed to {func}`matplotlib.pyplot.imshow`.

# Returns

axes_list

: A list of subplot axes that hold the images.

# See Also

plot_spectra : Plotting of multiple spectra
plot_signals : Plotting of multiple signals
plot_histograms : Compare signal histograms

# References

[^footnote-1]: Matplotlib colors API: <https://matplotlib.org/stable/api/colors_api.html>.

# Notes

`interpolation` is a useful parameter to provide as a keyword
argument to control how the space between pixels is interpolated. A
value of `'nearest'` will cause no interpolation between pixels.

`tight_layout` is known to be quite brittle, so an option is provided
to disable it. Turn this option off if output is not as expected,
or try adjusting `label`, `labelwrap`, or `per_row`.

functions.name: hyperspy.drawing.utils.make_cmap
documentation:
Create a matplotlib colormap with customized colors, optionally registering
it with matplotlib for simplified use.

Adapted from Chris Slocum's code at:
https://github.com/CSlocumWX/custom_colormap/blob/master/custom_colormaps/custom_colormaps.py
and used under the terms of that code's BSD-3 license

Parameters
----------
colors : iterable
    list of either tuples containing rgb values, or html strings
    Colors should be arranged so that the first color is the lowest
    value for the colorbar and the last is the highest.
name : str, optional
    Name of colormap to use when registering with matplotlib. Default
    'my_colormap'.
position : None, iterable, optional
    List containing the values (from [0,1]) that dictate the position
    of each color within the colormap. If None (default), the colors
    will be equally-spaced within the colorbar.
bit : bool, optional
    True if RGB colors are given in 8-bit [0 to 255] or False if given
    in arithmetic basis [0 to 1] (default).
register : bool, optional
    Switch to control whether or not to register the custom colormap
    with matplotlib in order to enable use by just the name string.
    Default True.

functions.name: hyperspy.drawing.utils.plot_spectra
documentation:
Plot several spectra in the same figure.

# Parameters

spectra

: Ordered spectra list of signal to plot. If `style` is "cascade" or
  "mosaic", the spectra can have different size and axes.
  For {class}`~.api.signals.BaseSignal` with navigation dimensions 1
  and signal dimension 0, the signal will be transposed to form a
  {class}`~.api.signals.Signal1D`.

style

: The style of the plot: 'overlap' (default), 'cascade', 'mosaic', or
  'heatmap'.

color

: Sets the color of the lines of the plots (no action on 'heatmap').
  For a list, if its length is less than the number of spectra to plot,
  the colors will be cycled. If `None` (default), use default matplotlib
  color cycle.

linestyle

: Sets the line style of the plots (no action on 'heatmap').
  The main line style are `'-'`, `'--'`, `'-.'`, `':'`.
  For a list, if its length is less than the number of
  spectra to plot, linestyle will be cycled.
  If `None`, use continuous lines (same as `'-'`).

drawstyle

: default 'default'
  The drawstyle determines how the points are connected, no action with
  `style='heatmap'`. See
  {meth}`matplotlib.lines.Line2D.set_drawstyle` for more information.
  The `'default'` value is defined by matplotlib.

padding

: Option for "cascade". 1 guarantees that there is no overlapping.
  However, in many cases, a value between 0 and 1 can produce a tighter
  plot without overlapping. Negative values have the same effect but
  reverse the order of the spectra without reversing the order of the
  colors.

legend

: If list of string, legend for 'cascade' or title for 'mosaic' is
  displayed. If 'auto', the title of each spectra (metadata.General.title)
  is used. Default None.

legend_picking

: If True (default), a spectrum can be toggled on and off by clicking on
  the legended line.

legend_loc

: This parameter controls where the legend is placed on the figure;
  see the pyplot.legend docstring for valid values. Default `'upper right'`.

fig

: If None (default), a default figure will be created.
  Not supported for the `'heatmap'` style.

ax

: If None (default), matplotlib axes will be created when necessary.
  Not supported for the `'heatmap'` style.

auto_update

: If True, the plot will update when the data are changed. Only supported
  with style='overlap' and a list of signal with navigation dimension 0.
  If None (default), update the plot only for style='overlap'.

normalise

: If True, the data are normalised to the [0, 1] interval in the plot.

kwargs

: Depending on the style used, the keyword arguments are passed to different functions

  - `"overlap"`, `"cascade"` or `"mosiac"`: arguments passed to {func}`matplotlib.pyplot.figure`
  - `"heatmap"`: arguments passed to {meth}`~.api.signals.Signal2D.plot`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> s = hs.signals.Signal1D(np.arange(100).reshape((10, 10)))
>>> hs.plot.plot_spectra(s, style='cascade', color='red', padding=0.5)
<Axes: xlabel='<undefined> (<undefined>)'>
```

To save the plot as a png-file

```pycon
>>> ax = hs.plot.plot_spectra(s)
>>> ax.figure.savefig("test.png") # doctest: +SKIP
```

# Returns

{class}`matplotlib.axes.Axes` or list of {class}`matplotlib.axes.Axes`

: An array is returned when `style` is 'mosaic'.

functions.name: hyperspy.drawing.utils.animate_legend
documentation:
Animate the legend of a figure.

A spectrum or image can be toggled on and off by clicking on the line in
the legend.

Parameters
----------

fig: None, matplotlib.figure, optional
    If None (default), pick the current figure using "plt.gcf".
ax:  {None, matplotlib.axes}, optional
    If None (Default), pick the current axes using "plt.gca".

Notes
-----
Code inspired from legend_picking.py in the matplotlib gallery.

functions.name: hyperspy.drawing.utils.plot_histograms
documentation:
Plot the histogram of every signal in the list in one figure.

This function creates a histogram for each signal and plots the list with
the {func}`~.api.plot.plot_spectra` function.

# Parameters

signal_list

: Ordered list of spectra to plot. If `style` is `"cascade"` or
  `"mosaic"`, the spectra can have different size and axes.

\%s
\%s
color : None, (list of) matplotlib color, optional

> Sets the color of the lines of the plots. For a list, if its length is
> less than the number of spectra to plot, the colors will be cycled.
> If `None`, use default matplotlib color cycle.

linestyle

: The main line styles are `'-'`, `'--'`, `'-.'`, `':'`.
  For a list, if its length is less than the number of
  spectra to plot, linestyle will be cycled.
  If `None`, use continuous lines (same as `'-'`).

legend

: Display a legend. If 'auto', the title of each spectra
  (metadata.General.title) is used.

legend_picking

: If True, a spectrum can be toggled on and off by clicking on
  the line in the legend.

fig

: If None (default), a default figure will be created.

kwargs

: other keyword arguments (weight and density) are described in
  {func}`numpy.histogram`.

# Examples

Histograms of two random chi-square distributions.

```pycon
>>> img = hs.signals.Signal2D(np.random.chisquare(1, [10, 10, 100]))
>>> img2 = hs.signals.Signal2D(np.random.chisquare(2, [10, 10, 100]))
>>> hs.plot.plot_histograms([img, img2], legend=['hist1', 'hist2'])
<Axes: xlabel='value (<undefined>)', ylabel='Intensity'>
```

# Returns

matplotlib.axes.Axes or list of matplotlib.axes.Axes

: An array is returned when `style='mosaic'`.

functions.name: hyperspy.drawing.utils._create_span_roi_group
documentation:
Creates a set of `N` of {py:class}`~.roi.SpanROI`s that sit along axis at sensible positions.

# Arguments

sig_ax: DataAxis

: The axis over which the ROI will be placed

N: int

: The number of ROIs

functions.name: hyperspy.drawing.utils._create_rect_roi_group
documentation:
Creates a set of `N` {py:class}`~roi.RectangularROI`s that sit along
`waxis` and `haxis` at sensible positions.

# Arguments

sig_wax: DataAxis

: The width axis over which the ROI will be placed

sig_hax: DataAxis

: The height axis over which the ROI will be placed

N: int

: The number of ROIs

functions.name: hyperspy.drawing.utils.plot_roi_map
documentation:
Plot one or multiple ROI maps of a `signal`.

Uses regions of interest (ROIs) to select ranges along the signal axis.

For each ROI, a plot is generated of the summed data values within
this signal ROI at each point in the `signal`'s navigation space.

The ROIs can be moved interactively and the corresponding map plots will
update automatically.

# Parameters

signal

: The signal to inspect.

rois

: ROIs to slice the signal in signal space. If `int`, define the number of
  ROIs to use.

color

: Color of the ROIs. Any string supported by matplotlib to define a color
  can be used. The length of the list must be equal to the number ROIs.
  If None (default), the default matplotlib colors are used.

cmap

: Only for signals with navigation dimension of 2. Define the colormap of the map(s).
  If string, any string supported by matplotlib to define a colormap can be used
  and a colored frame matching the ROI color will be added to the map.
  If list of str, it must be the same length as the ROIs.
  If None (default), the colors from the `color` argument are used and no colored
  frame is added.

single_figure

: Whether to plot on a single figure or several figures.
  If True, {func}`~.api.plot.plot_images` or {func}`~.plot.plot_spectra`
  will be used, depending on the navigation dimension of the signal.

single_figure_kwargs

: Only when `single_figure=True`. Keywords arguments are passed to
  {func}`~.api.plot.plot_images` or {func}`~.plot.plot_spectra`
  depending on the navigation dimension of the signal.
  If None, default `kwargs` are used with the following changes
  `scalebar=[0]`, `axes_decor="off"` and `suptitle=""`.

kwargs

: The keyword argument are passed to {meth}`~.api.signals.Signal2D.plot`.

# Returns

rois

: The ROI objects that slice `signal`.

roi_sums

: The sums of the signals defined by the ROIs.

# Notes

Performance consideration:

- {class}`~.api.roi.RectangularROI` is ~2x faster than {class}`~.api.roi.CircleROI`.
- If the data sliced by the ROI contains {obj}`numpy.nan`, {func}`numpy.nansum`
  will be used instead of {func}`numpy.sum` at the cost of a speed penalty (more than
  2 times slower).
- Plotting ROI maps on a single figure is slower than on separate figures.

# Examples

**3D hyperspectral data**

For 3D hyperspectral data, the ROIs used will be instances of
{class}`~.api.roi.SpanROI`. Therefore, these ROIs can be used to select
particular spectral ranges, e.g. a particular peak.

The map generated for a given ROI is therefore the sum of this spectral
region at each point in the hyperspectral map. Therefore, regions of the
sample where this peak is bright will be bright in this map.

**4D STEM**

For 4D STEM data, by default, the ROIs used will be instances of
{class}`~.api.roi.RectangularROI`. Other hyperspy ROIs, such as
{class}`~.api.roi.CircleROI` can be used. These ROIs can be used
to select particular regions in reciprocal space, e.g. a particular
diffraction spot.

The map generated for a given ROI is the intensity of this
region at each point in the scan. Therefore, regions of the
scan where a particular spot is intense will appear bright.

classes.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.WidgetBase

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.WidgetBase.is_on
documentation: Determines if the widget is set to draw if valid (turned on).

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.WidgetBase.set_on
documentation:
Change the on state of the widget. If turning off, all patches will
be removed from the matplotlib axes and the widget will disconnect from
all events. If turning on, the patch(es) will be added to the
matplotlib axes, and the widget will connect to its default events.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.WidgetBase._set_patch
documentation: Create the matplotlib patch(es), and store it in self.patch

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.WidgetBase._add_patch_to
documentation: Create and add the matplotlib patches to 'ax'

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.WidgetBase.set_mpl_ax
documentation:
Set the matplotlib Axes that the widget will draw to. If the widget
on state is True, it will also add the patch to the Axes, and connect
to its default events.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.WidgetBase.select
documentation:
Cause this widget to be the selected widget in its MPL axes. This
assumes that the widget has its patch added to the MPL axes.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.WidgetBase.connect
documentation: Connect to the matplotlib Axes' events.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.WidgetBase.connect_navigate
documentation:
Connect to the axes_manager such that changes in the widget or in
the axes_manager are reflected in the other.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.WidgetBase.disconnect_navigate
documentation: Disconnect a previous naivgation connection.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.WidgetBase._on_navigate
documentation: Callback for axes_manager's change notification.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.WidgetBase.disconnect
documentation: Disconnect from all events (both matplotlib and navigation).

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.WidgetBase.close
documentation:
Set the on state to off (removes patch and disconnects), and trigger
events.closed.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.WidgetBase.draw_patch
documentation: Update the patch drawing.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.WidgetBase._v2i
documentation:
Wrapped version of DataAxis.value2index, which bounds the index
between axis.low_index and axis.high_index+1, and does not raise a
ValueError.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.WidgetBase._i2v
documentation:
Wrapped version of DataAxis.index2value, which bounds the value
inbetween axis.low_value and axis.high_value + axis.scale when the axis
is uniform and does not raise a ValueError.

Base class for interactive widgets/patches. A widget creates and
maintains one or more matplotlib patches, and manages the interaction code
so that the user can manipulate it on the fly.

This base class implements functionality which is common to all such
widgets, mainly the code that manages the patch, axes management, and
sets up common events ('changed' and 'closed').

Any inherting subclasses must implement the following methods:
    _set_patch(self)
    _on_navigate(obj, name, old, new)  # Only for widgets that can navigate

It should also make sure to fill the 'axes' attribute as early as
possible (but after the base class init), so that it is available when
needed.

classes.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.DraggableWidgetBase

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.DraggableWidgetBase._get_indices
documentation: Returns a tuple with the position (indices).

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.DraggableWidgetBase._set_indices
documentation:
Sets the position of the widget (by indices). The dimensions should
correspond to that of the 'axes' attribute. Calls _pos_changed if the
value has changed, which is then responsible for triggering any
relevant events.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.DraggableWidgetBase._pos_changed
documentation:
Call when the position of the widget has changed. It triggers the
relevant events, and updates the patch position.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.DraggableWidgetBase._validate_pos
documentation:
Validates the passed position. Depending on the position and the
implementation, this can either fire a ValueError, or return a modified
position that has valid values. Or simply return the unmodified
position if everything is ok.

This default implementation bounds the position within the axes limits.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.DraggableWidgetBase._get_position
documentation: Provides the position of the widget (by values) in a tuple.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.DraggableWidgetBase._set_position
documentation:
Sets the position of the widget (by values). The dimensions should
correspond to that of the 'axes' attribute. Calls _pos_changed if the
value has changed, which is then responsible for triggering any
relevant events.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.DraggableWidgetBase._do_snap_position
documentation:
Snaps position to axes grid. Returns snapped value. If value is
passed as an argument, the internal state is left untouched, if not
the position attribute is updated to the snapped value.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.DraggableWidgetBase._onjumpclick
documentation: This method must be provided by subclasses

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.DraggableWidgetBase._onmousemove
documentation:
Callback for mouse movement. For dragging, the implementor would
normally check that the widget is picked, and that the event.inaxes
Axes equals self.ax.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.DraggableWidgetBase._update_patch_position
documentation: Updates the position of the patch on the plot.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.DraggableWidgetBase._update_patch_geometry
documentation: Updates all geometrical properties of the patch on the plot.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.DraggableWidgetBase.button_release
documentation: whenever a mouse button is released.

Adds the `position` and `indices` properties, and adds a framework for
letting the user drag the patch around. Also adds the `moved` event.

The default behavior is that `position` snaps to the values corresponding
to the values of the axes grid (i.e. no subpixel values). This behavior
can be controlled by the property `snap_position`.

Any inheritors must override these methods:
    _onmousemove(self, event)
    _update_patch_position(self)
    _set_patch(self)

classes.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.Widget1DBase
documentation:
A base class for 1D widgets.

It sets the right dimensions for size and
position, adds the 'border_thickness' attribute and initalizes the 'axes'
attribute to the first two navigation axes if possible, if not, the two
first signal_axes are used. Other than that, it mainly supplies common
utility functions for inheritors, and implements required functions for
ResizableDraggableWidgetBase.

The implementation for ResizableDraggableWidgetBase methods all assume that
a Rectangle patch will be used, centered on position. If not, the
inheriting class will have to override those as applicable.

classes.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.ResizableDraggableWidgetBase

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.ResizableDraggableWidgetBase._get_step
documentation:
Use to determine the size of the widget with support for non
uniform axis.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.ResizableDraggableWidgetBase._get_size
documentation:
Getter for 'size' property. Returns the size as a tuple (to prevent
unintended in-place changes).

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.ResizableDraggableWidgetBase._set_size
documentation:
Setter for the 'size' property.

Calls _size_changed to handle size change, if the value has changed.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.ResizableDraggableWidgetBase.increase_size
documentation: Increment all sizes by 1. Applied via 'size' property.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.ResizableDraggableWidgetBase.decrease_size
documentation: Decrement all sizes by 1. Applied via 'size' property.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.ResizableDraggableWidgetBase._size_changed
documentation: Triggers resize and changed events, and updates the patch.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.ResizableDraggableWidgetBase.get_size_in_indices
documentation:
Gets the size property converted to the index space (via 'axes'
attribute).

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.ResizableDraggableWidgetBase.set_size_in_indices
documentation:
Sets the size property converted to the index space (via 'axes'
attribute).

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.ResizableDraggableWidgetBase.get_centre
documentation:
Gets the center indices. The default implementation is simply the
position + half the size in axes space, which should work for any
symmetric widget, but more advanced widgets will need to decide whether
to return the center of gravity or the geometrical center of the
bounds.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.ResizableDraggableWidgetBase.get_centre_index
documentation:
Gets the center position (in index space). The default
implementation is simply the indices + half the size, which should
work for any symmetric widget, but more advanced widgets will need to
decide whether to return the center of gravity or the geometrical
center of the bounds.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.ResizableDraggableWidgetBase._update_patch_size
documentation: Updates the size of the patch on the plot.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.ResizableDraggableWidgetBase._update_patch_geometry
documentation: Updates all geometry of the patch on the plot.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.ResizableDraggableWidgetBase._apply_changes
documentation:
Evaluates whether the widget has been moved/resized, and triggers
the correct events and updates the patch geometry. This function has
the advantage that the geometry is updated only once, preventing
flickering, and the 'changed' event only fires once.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.ResizableDraggableWidgetBase.button_release
documentation: whenever a mouse button is released.

Adds the `size` property and get_size_in_axes method, and adds a
framework for letting the user resize the patch, including resizing by
key strokes ('+', '-'). Also adds the 'resized' event.

Utility functions for resizing are implemented by `increase_size` and
`decrease_size`, which will in-/decrement the size by 1. Other utility
functions include `get_centre` and `get_centre_indices` which returns the
center position, and the internal _apply_changes which helps make sure that
only one 'changed' event is fired for a combined move and resize.

Any inheritors must override these methods:
    _update_patch_position(self)
    _update_patch_size(self)
    _update_patch_geometry(self)
    _set_patch(self)

classes.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.Widget2DBase

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.Widget2DBase._get_patch_xy
documentation:
Returns the xy position of the widget. In this default
implementation, the widget is centered on the position.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.Widget2DBase._get_patch_bounds
documentation:
Returns the bounds of the patch in the form of a tuple in the order
left, top, width, height. In matplotlib, 'bottom' is used instead of
'top' as the naming assumes an upwards pointing y-axis, meaning the
lowest value corresponds to bottom. However, our widgets will normally
only go on images (which has an inverted y-axis in MPL by default), so
we define the lowest value to be termed 'top'.

A base class for 2D widgets. It sets the right dimensions for size and
position, adds the 'border_thickness' attribute and initalizes the 'axes'
attribute to the first two navigation axes if possible, if not, the two
first signal_axes are used. Other than that it mainly supplies common
utility functions for inheritors, and implements required functions for
ResizableDraggableWidgetBase.

The implementation for ResizableDraggableWidgetBase methods all assume that
a Rectangle patch will be used, centered on position. If not, the
inheriting class will have to override those as applicable.

classes.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.ResizersMixin

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.ResizersMixin._update_resizers
documentation: Update resizer handles' patch geometry.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.ResizersMixin._set_resizers
documentation:
Turns the resizers on/off, in much the same way that _set_patch
works.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.ResizersMixin._get_resizer_size
documentation:
Gets the size of the resizer handles in axes coordinates. If
'resize_pixel_size' is None, a size of one pixel will be used.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.ResizersMixin._get_resizer_offset
documentation:
Utility for getting the distance from the boundary box to the
center of the resize handles.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.ResizersMixin._get_resizer_pos
documentation: Get the positions of the resizer handles.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.ResizersMixin._set_patch
documentation:
Creates the resizer handles, regardless of whether they will be
used or not.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.ResizersMixin.set_on
documentation: Turns on/off resizers when widget is turned on/off.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.ResizersMixin.onpick
documentation:
Picking of main patch is same as for widget base, but this also
handles picking of the resize handles. If a resize handle is picked,
`picked` is set to `True`, and `resizer_picked` is set to an integer
indicating which handle was picked (0-3 for top left, top right, bottom
left, bottom right). It is set to `False` if another widget was picked.

If the main patch is picked, the offset from the picked pixel to the
`position` is stored in `pick_offset`. This can be used in e.g.
`_onmousemove` to ease dragging code (prevent widget center/corner
snapping to mouse).

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.widget.ResizersMixin._add_patch_to
documentation:
Same as widget base, but also adds resizers if 'resizers' property
is True.

Widget mix-in for adding resizing manipulation handles.

The default handles are green boxes displayed on the outside corners of the
boundaries. By default, the handles are only displayed when the widget is
selected (`picked` in matplotlib terminology).

Attributes
----------
resizers : bool
    Property that determines whether the resizer handles should be used
resize_color : matplotlib color
    The color of the resize handles.
resize_pixel_size : tuple or None
    Size of the resize handles in screen pixels. If None, it is set
    equal to the size of one 'data-pixel' (image pixel size).
resizer_picked : False or int
    Inidcates which, if any, resizer was selected the last time the
    widget was picked. `False` if another patch was picked, or the
    index of the resizer handle that was picked.

classes.name: hyperspy.drawing._markers.polygons.Polygons

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing._markers.polygons.Polygons.__init__
documentation:
Initialize the set of Segments Markers.

# Parameters

verts

: The verts define the vertices of the polygons. Note that this can be
  a ragged list and as such it is not automatically cast to a numpy
  array as that would result in an array of objects.
  In the form \[\[[x1,y1], [x2,y2], ... [xn, yn]\],\[[x1,y1], [x2,y2], ...[xm,ym]\], ...\].

kwargs

: Additional keyword arguments are passed to
  {class}`matplotlib.collections.PolyCollection`

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import hyperspy.api as hs
>>> import numpy as np
>>> # Create a Signal2D with 2 navigation dimensions
>>> data = np.ones((25, 25, 100, 100))
>>> s = hs.signals.Signal2D(data)
>>> poylgon1 = [[1, 1], [20, 20], [1, 20], [25, 5]]
>>> poylgon2 = [[50, 60], [90, 40], [60, 40], [23, 60]]
>>> verts = [poylgon1, poylgon2]
>>> # Create the markers
>>> m = hs.plot.markers.Polygons(
...     verts=verts,
...     linewidth=3,
...     facecolors=('g',),
...     )
>>> # Add the marker to the signal
>>> s.plot()
>>> s.add_marker(m)
```

# Notes

Unlike markers using `offsets` argument, the positions of the polygon
are defined by the `verts` argument and the tranform specifying the
coordinate system of the `verts` is `transform`.
A Collection of Rectangles Markers

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing._markers.vertical_lines.VerticalLines.__init__
documentation:
Initialize the set of Vertical Line Markers.

# Parameters

x: [n]

: Positions of the markers

kwargs: dict

: Keyword arguments passed to the underlying marker collection. Any argument
  that is array-like and has `dtype=object` is assumed to be an iterating
  argument and is treated as such.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import hyperspy.api as hs
>>> import numpy as np
>>> # Create a Signal2D with 2 navigation dimensions
>>> rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
>>> data = rng.random((25, 25, 100))
>>> s = hs.signals.Signal1D(data)
>>> offsets = np.array([10, 20, 40])
>>> # Create the markers
>>> m = hs.plot.markers.VerticalLines(
...     offsets=offsets,
...     linewidth=3,
...     colors=['r', 'g', 'b'],
...     )
>>> # Add the marker to the signal
>>> s.plot()
>>> s.add_marker(m)
```
A set of Vertical Line Markers

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing.mpl_he.MPL_HyperExplorer.close
documentation:
Close the plot of the signals.

This function is called programmatically or on matplotlib close
callback.
When closing, it does the following:
1. trigger a closed event
2. disconnect the closed event
3. run the close method of the signal_plot and navigator_plot
4. reset the attribute

Returns the angle in radians between the vectors 'v1' and 'v2'.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> print(angle_between((1, 0), (0, 1)))
1.5707963267948966
>>> print(angle_between((1, 0), (1, 0)))
0.0
```

documentation: Get interaction function from pixel position (cx,cy)

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing._widgets.line2d.Line2DWidget.onpick
documentation:
Pick, and if picked, figure out which function to apply. Also store
pouse position for use by _onmousemove. As rotation does not work very
well with incremental rotations, the original points are stored if
we're rotating.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing._widgets.line2d.Line2DWidget._onmousemove
documentation: Delegate to _move(), _resize() or _rotate().

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing._widgets.line2d.Line2DWidget._get_diff
documentation:
Get difference in position in event and what is stored in _prev_pos,
in value space.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing._widgets.line2d.Line2DWidget._move
documentation:
Move line by drag start position + difference in mouse post from
when dragging started (picked).

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing._widgets.line2d.Line2DWidget._resize
documentation:
Move vertex by difference from pick / last mouse move. Update
'_prev_pos'.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing._widgets.line2d.Line2DWidget._rotate
documentation:
Rotate original points by the angle between mouse position and
rotation start position (rotation center = line center).

class_variables.name: hyperspy.drawing._widgets.line2d.Line2DWidget.FUNC_NONE
documentation: Do nothing

class_variables.name: hyperspy.drawing._widgets.line2d.Line2DWidget.FUNC_MOVE
documentation: Move the widget

class_variables.name: hyperspy.drawing._widgets.line2d.Line2DWidget.FUNC_RESIZE
documentation: Move a vertex

class_variables.name: hyperspy.drawing._widgets.line2d.Line2DWidget.FUNC_ROTATE
documentation: Rotate

class_variables.name: hyperspy.drawing._widgets.line2d.Line2DWidget.FUNC_SIZERS
documentation: Change linewidth by indicators

class_variables.name: hyperspy.drawing._widgets.line2d.Line2DWidget.FUNC_A
documentation: Resize/rotate by first vertex

class_variables.name: hyperspy.drawing._widgets.line2d.Line2DWidget.FUNC_B
documentation: Resize/rotate by second vertex

A free-form line on a 2D plot. Enables dragging and moving the end
points, but also allows rotation of the widget by moving the mouse beyond
the end points of the line.

The widget adds the 'linewidth' attribute, which is different from the size
in the following regards: 'linewidth' is simply the width of the patch
drawn from point to point. If 'size' is greater than 1, it will in
principle select a rotated rectangle. If 'size' is greater than 4, the
bounds of this rectangle will be visualized by two dashed lines along the
outline of this rectangle, instead of a single line in the center.

The widget also adds the attributes 'radius_resize', 'radius_move' and
'radius_rotate' (defaults: 5, 5, 10), which determines the picker radius
for resizing, aka. moving the edge points (by picking within
'radius_resize' from an edge point); for moving (by picking within
'radius_move' from the body of the line); and for rotation (by picking
within 'radius_rotate' of the edge points on the "outside" of the line).
The priority is in the order resize, rotate, move; so the 'radius_rotate'
should always be larger than 'radius_resize' if the function is to be
accessible (putting it lower is an easy way to disable the functionality).


Notes
-----
This widget's internal position does not lock to axes points by default.

Notes
-----
The 'position' is now a 2D tuple: tuple(tuple(x1, x2), tuple(y1, y2))

Notes
-----
The 'size' property corresponds to line width, so it has a len() of only
one.

Returns a list where each entry contains a `(x, y)` tuple
of the vertices of the polygon. The polygon is not closed.
Returns an empty list if no polygon is set.

documentation:
Returns the xy coordinates of the patch centre. In this implementation, the
centre of the widget is the centre of the polygon's bounding box.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing._widgets.polygon.PolygonWidget.finished_building
documentation:
Function for checking if polygon is finished being built. Useful for finding
out if the vertices are complete.

PolygonWidget is a widget for drawing an arbitrary
polygon, which can then be used as a region-of-interest.
To construct the polygon, click at the positions to put the
vertices. Click the first vertex again to complete construction.
The polygon can be moved by pressing 'shift' and clicking.
A polygon vertex can be moved by clicking its handle. If incomplete,
it is also necessary to press 'ctrl'.
The polygon can be deleted by pressing 'esc'.

Sets the patch to a matplotlib Rectangle with the correct geometry.
The geometry is defined by _get_patch_xy, and get_size_in_axes.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing._widgets.rectangles.SquareWidget._onmousemove
documentation: on mouse motion move the patch if picked

SquareWidget is a symmetric, Rectangle-patch based widget, which can be
dragged, and resized by keystrokes/code. As the widget is normally only
meant to indicate position, the sizing is deemed purely visual, but there
is nothing that forces this use. However, it should be noted that the outer
bounds only correspond to pure indices for odd sizes.

classes.name: hyperspy.drawing._widgets.rectangles.RectangleWidget

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing._widgets.rectangles.RectangleWidget._parse_bounds_args
documentation:
Internal utility function to parse args/kwargs passed to set_bounds
and set_ibounds.
--------- External interface ---------

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing._widgets.rectangles.RectangleWidget.set_ibounds
documentation:
Set bounds by indices. Bounds can either be specified in order left,
bottom, width, height; or by keywords:

* 'bounds': tuple (left, top, width, height)

OR

* 'x'/'left'
* 'y'/'top'
* 'w'/'width', alternatively 'right'
* 'h'/'height', alternatively 'bottom'

If specifying with keywords, any unspecified dimensions will be kept
constant (note: width/height will be kept, not right/bottom).

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing._widgets.rectangles.RectangleWidget.set_bounds
documentation:
Set bounds by values. Bounds can either be specified in order left,
bottom, width, height; or by keywords:

* 'bounds': tuple (left, top, width, height)

OR

* 'x'/'left'
* 'y'/'top'
* 'w'/'width', alternatively 'right' (x+w)
* 'h'/'height', alternatively 'bottom' (y+h)

If specifying with keywords, any unspecified dimensions will be kept
constant (note: width/height will be kept, not right/bottom).

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing._widgets.rectangles.RectangleWidget._validate_pos
documentation: Constrict the position within bounds.

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing._widgets.rectangles.RectangleWidget._set_size
documentation: --------- Internal functions ---------
--- Internals that trigger events ---

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing._widgets.rectangles.RectangleWidget._get_patch_xy
documentation:
Get xy value for Rectangle with position being top left. This value
deviates from the 'position', as 'position' correspond to the center
value of the pixel. Here, xy corresponds to the top left of the pixel.
--- End internals that trigger events ---

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing._widgets.rectangles.RectangleWidget._validate_geometry
documentation:
Make sure the entire patch always stays within bounds. First the
position (either from position property or from x1/y1 arguments), is
limited within the bounds. Then, if the bottom/right edges are out of
bounds, the position is changed so that they will be at the limit.

The modified geometry is stored, but no change checks are performed.
Call _apply_changes after this in order to process any changes (the
size might change if it is set larger than the bounds size).

methods.name: hyperspy.drawing._widgets.rectangles.RectangleWidget._onmousemove
documentation: on mouse motion draw the cursor if picked

RectangleWidget is a asymmetric, Rectangle-patch based widget, which can
be dragged and resized by mouse/keys. For resizing by mouse, it adds a
small Rectangle patch on the outer border of the main patch, to serve as
resize handles. This feature can be enabled/disabled by the 'resizers'
property, and the size/color of the handles are set by
'resize_color'/'resize_pixel_size'.

For optimized changes of geometry, the class implements two methods
'set_bounds' and 'set_ibounds', to set the geomtry of the rectangle by
value and index space coordinates, respectivly. It also adds the 'width'
and 'height' properties for verbosity.

For keyboard resizing, 'x'/'c' and 'y'/'u' will increase/decrease the size
of the rectangle along the first and the second axis, respectively.

Implements the internal method _validate_geometry to make sure the patch
will always stay within bounds.

classes.name: hyperspy._components.exponential.Exponential

methods.name: hyperspy._components.exponential.Exponential.estimate_parameters
documentation:
Estimate the parameters for the exponential component by splitting
the signal window into two regions and using their geometric means

# Parameters

signal : {class}`~.api.signals.Signal1D`
x1 : float

> Defines the left limit of the spectral range to use for the
> estimation.

x2

: Defines the right limit of the spectral range to use for the
  estimation.

only_current

: If False estimates the parameters for the full dataset.

# Returns

bool

Exponential function component.

$$
f(x) = A\cdot\exp\left(-\frac{x}{\tau}\right)
$$

| Variable | Parameter |
| -------- | --------- |
| $A$      | A         |
| $\tau$   | tau       |

# Parameters

A: float

: Maximum intensity

tau: float

: Scale parameter (time constant)

kwargs

: Extra keyword arguments are passed to the
  {class}`~.api.model.components1D.Expression` component.

classes.name: hyperspy._components.gaussian.Gaussian

methods.name: hyperspy._components.gaussian.Gaussian.estimate_parameters
documentation:
Estimate the Gaussian by calculating the momenta.

# Parameters

signal : {class}`~.api.signals.Signal1D`
x1 : float

> Defines the left limit of the spectral range to use for the
> estimation.

x2

: Defines the right limit of the spectral range to use for the
  estimation.

only_current

: If False estimates the parameters for the full dataset.

# Returns

bool

# Notes

Adapted from <https://scipy-cookbook.readthedocs.io/items/FittingData.html>

# Examples

```pycon
>>> g = hs.model.components1D.Gaussian()
>>> x = np.arange(-10, 10, 0.01)
>>> data = np.zeros((32, 32, 2000))
>>> data[:] = g.function(x).reshape((1, 1, 2000))
>>> s = hs.signals.Signal1D(data)
>>> s.axes_manager[-1].offset = -10
>>> s.axes_manager[-1].scale = 0.01
>>> g.estimate_parameters(s, -10, 10, False)
True
```

Normalized Gaussian function component.

$$
f(x) = \frac{A}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\left[
       -\frac{\left(x-x_0\right)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right]
$$

| Variable | Parameter |
| -------- | --------- |
| $A$      | A         |
| $\sigma$ | sigma     |
| $x_0$    | centre    |

# Parameters

A

: Area, equals height scaled by $\sigma\sqrt{(2\pi)}$.
  `GaussianHF` implements the Gaussian function with a height parameter
  corresponding to the peak height.

sigma

: Scale parameter of the Gaussian distribution.

centre

: Location of the Gaussian maximum (peak position).

kwargs

: Extra keyword arguments are passed to the
  {class}`~.api.model.components1D.Expression` component.

# Attributes

fwhm

: Convenience attribute to get and set the full width at half maximum.

height

: Convenience attribute to get and set the height.

# See Also

GaussianHF

methods.name: hyperspy._components.gaussianhf.GaussianHF.integral_as_signal
documentation: Utility function to get gaussian integral as Signal1D

Normalized gaussian function component, with a `fwhm` parameter
instead of the `sigma` parameter, and a `height` parameter instead of
the area parameter `A` (scaling difference of
$\sigma \sqrt{\left(2\pi\right)}$).
This makes the parameter vs. peak maximum independent of $\sigma$,
and thereby makes locking of the parameter more viable. As long as there
is no binning, the `height` parameter corresponds directly to the peak
maximum, if not, the value is scaled by a linear constant
(`signal_axis.scale`).

$$
f(x) = h\cdot\exp{\left[-\frac{4 \log{2}
    \left(x-c\right)^{2}}{W^{2}}\right]}
$$

| Variable | Parameter |
| -------- | --------- |
| $h$      | height    |
| $W$      | fwhm      |
| $c$      | centre    |

# Parameters

height: float

: The height of the peak. If there is no binning, this corresponds
  directly to the maximum, otherwise the maximum divided by
  signal_axis.scale

fwhm: float

: The full width half maximum value, i.e. the width of the gaussian
  at half the value of gaussian peak (at centre).

centre: float

: Location of the gaussian maximum, also the mean position.

kwargs

: Extra keyword arguments are passed to the
  {class}`~.api.model.components1D.Expression` component.

# Attributes

A

: Convenience attribute to get, set the area and defined for
  compatibility with `Gaussian` component.

sigma

: Convenience attribute to get, set the width and defined for
  compatibility with `Gaussian` component.

# See Also

Gaussian

documentation: float: Height of the Gaussian

methods.name: hyperspy._components.gaussian2d.Gaussian2D.rotation_major_axis
documentation:
float: Rotation angle in radian between the major axis (axis with
the largest sigma value) and the horizontal axis.
Only for Gaussian2D component created with `add_rotation=True`.

Normalized 2D elliptical Gaussian function component.

$$
f(x,y) = \frac{A}{2\pi s_x s_y}\exp\left[-\frac{\left(x-x_0\right)
^{2}}{2s_{x}^{2}}\frac{\left(y-y_0\right)^{2}}{2s_{y}^{2}}\right]
$$

| Variable  | Parameter  |
| --------- | ---------- |
| $A$       | A          |
| $s_x,s_y$ | sigma_x/y  |
| $x_0,y_0$ | centre_x/y |

# Parameters

A

: Volume (height of the peak scaled by $2 \pi s_x s_y$) --
  eqivalent to the area in a 1D Gaussian.

sigma_x

: Width (scale parameter) of the Gaussian distribution in `x` direction.

sigma_y

: Width (scale parameter) of the Gaussian distribution in `y` direction.

centre_x

: Location of the Gaussian maximum (peak position) in `x` direction.

centre_x

: Location of the Gaussian maximum (peak position) in `y` direction.

add_rotation

: If True, add the parameter `rotation_angle` corresponding to the angle
  between the `x` and the horizontal axis.

kwargs

: Extra keyword arguments are passed to the
  {class}`~.api.model.components1D.Expression` component.

# Attributes

fwhm_x, fwhm_y

: Convenience attributes to get and set the full width at half maximum along
  the two axes.

classes.name: hyperspy._components.arctan.Arctan
documentation:
Arctan function component.

$$
f(x) = A \cdot \arctan \left[ k \left( x-x_0 \right) \right]
$$

| Variable | Parameter |
| -------- | --------- |
| $A$      | A         |
| $k$      | k         |
| $x_0$    | x0        |

# Parameters

A

: Amplitude parameter. $\lim_{x\to -\infty}f(x)=-A$ and
  $\lim_{x\to\infty}f(x)=A$

k

: Slope (steepness of the step). The larger $k$, the sharper the
  step.

x0

: Center parameter (position of zero crossing $f(x_0)=0$).

classes.name: hyperspy._components.heaviside.HeavisideStep
documentation:
The Heaviside step function.

Based on the corresponding [numpy function](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.heaviside.html)
using the half maximum definition for the central point:

$$
f(x) =
\begin{cases}
0 & x<n\\
A/2 & x=n\\
A & x>n
\end{cases}
$$

| Variable | Parameter |
| -------- | --------- |
| $n$      | centre    |
| $A$      | height    |

# Parameters

n

: Location parameter defining the x position of the step.

A

: Height parameter for x>n.

kwargs

: Extra keyword arguments are passed to the
  {class}`~.api.model.components1D.Expression` component.

classes.name: hyperspy._components.skew_normal.SkewNormal

methods.name: hyperspy._components.skew_normal.SkewNormal.estimate_parameters
documentation:
Estimate the skew normal distribution by calculating the momenta.

# Parameters

signal : {class}`~.api.signals.Signal1D`
x1 : float

> Defines the left limit of the spectral range to use for the
> estimation.

x2

: Defines the right limit of the spectral range to use for the
  estimation.

only_current

: If False estimates the parameters for the full dataset.

# Returns

bool

# Notes

Adapted from Lin, Lee and Yen, Statistica Sinica 17, 909-927 (2007)
<https://www.jstor.org/stable/24307705>

# Examples

```pycon
>>> g = hs.model.components1D.SkewNormal()
>>> x = np.arange(-10, 10, 0.01)
>>> data = np.zeros((32, 32, 2000))
>>> data[:] = g.function(x).reshape((1, 1, 2000))
>>> s = hs.signals.Signal1D(data)
>>> s.axes_manager._axes[-1].offset = -10
>>> s.axes_manager._axes[-1].scale = 0.01
>>> g.estimate_parameters(s, -10, 10, False)
True
```

methods.name: hyperspy._components.skew_normal.SkewNormal.mean
documentation: Mean (position) of the component.

methods.name: hyperspy._components.skew_normal.SkewNormal.variance
documentation: Variance of the component.

methods.name: hyperspy._components.skew_normal.SkewNormal.skewness
documentation: Skewness of the component.

methods.name: hyperspy._components.skew_normal.SkewNormal.mode
documentation: Mode (position of maximum) of the component.

Skew normal distribution component.

Asymmetric peak shape based on a normal distribution.

For definition see


<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Skew_normal_distribution>

See also 

<http://azzalini.stat.unipd.it/SN/>

$$
f(x) &= 2 A \phi(x) \Phi(x) \\
\phi(x) &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\mathrm{exp}{\left[
           -\frac{t(x)^2}{2}\right]} \\
\Phi(x) &= \frac{1}{2}\left[1 + \mathrm{erf}\left(\frac{
           \alpha~t(x)}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right] \\
t(x) &= \frac{x-x_0}{\omega}
$$

| Variable | Parameter |
| -------- | --------- |
| $x_0$    | x0        |
| $A$      | A         |
| $\omega$ | scale     |
| $\alpha$ | shape     |

# Parameters

x0

: Location of the peak position (not maximum, which is given by
  the `mode` property).

A

: Height parameter of the peak.

scale

: Width (sigma) parameter.

shape: float

: Skewness (asymmetry) parameter. For shape=0, the normal
  distribution (Gaussian) is obtained. The distribution is
  right skewed (longer tail to the right) if shape>0 and is
  left skewed if shape\<0.

kwargs

: Extra keyword arguments are passed to the
  {class}`~.api.model.components1D.Expression` component.

# Notes

The properties `mean` (position), `variance`, `skewness` and `mode`
(position of maximum) are defined for convenience.

methods.name: hyperspy._components.expression.Expression._compute_expression_part
documentation: Compute the expression for a given value or map["values"].

Create a component from a string expression.

It automatically generates the partial derivatives and the
class docstring.

# Parameters

expression

: Component function in SymPy text expression format with
  substitutions separated by `;`. See examples and the SymPy
  documentation for details. In order to vary the components along the
  signal dimensions, the variables `x` and `y` must be included for 1D
  or 2D components. Also, if `module` is "numexpr" the
  functions are limited to those that numexpr support. See its
  documentation for details.

name

: Name of the component.

position

: The parameter name that defines the position of the component if
  applicable. It enables interative adjustment of the position of the
  component in the model. For 2D components, a tuple must be passed
  with the name of the two parameters e.g. `("x0", "y0")`.

module

: Module used to evaluate the function. numexpr is often faster but
  it supports fewer functions and requires installing numexpr.
  If None, the "numexpr" will be used if installed.

add_rotation

: This is only relevant for 2D components. If `True` it automatically
  adds `rotation_angle` parameter.

rotation_center

: If None, the rotation center is the center i.e. (0, 0) if `position`
  is not defined, otherwise the center is the coordinates specified
  by `position`. Alternatively a tuple with the (x, y) coordinates
  of the center can be provided.

rename_pars

: The desired name of a parameter may sometimes coincide with e.g.
  the name of a scientific function, what prevents using it in the
  `expression`. `rename_parameters` is a dictionary to map the name
  of the parameter in the `` expression` `` to the desired name of the
  parameter in the `Component`. For example: {"\_gamma": "gamma"}.

compute_gradients

: If `True`, compute the gradient automatically using sympy. If sympy
  does not support the calculation of the partial derivatives, for
  example in case of expression containing a "where" condition,
  it can be disabled by using `compute_gradients=False`.

linear_parameter_list

: A list of the components parameters that are known to be linear
  parameters.

check_parameter_linearity

: If `True`, automatically check if each parameter is linear and set
  its corresponding attribute accordingly. If `False`, the default is to
  set all parameters, except for those who are specified in
  `linear_parameter_list`.

kwargs

: Keyword arguments can be used to initialise the value of the
  parameters.

# Notes

As of version 1.4, Sympy's lambdify function, that the
{class}`~.api.model.components1D.Expression`
components uses internally, does not support the differentiation of
some expressions, for example those containing a "where" condition.
In such cases, the gradients can be set manually if required.

# Examples

The following creates a Gaussian component and set the initial value
of the parameters:

```pycon
>>> hs.model.components1D.Expression(
... expression="height * exp(-(x - x0) ** 2 * 4 * log(2)/ fwhm ** 2)",
... name="Gaussian",
... height=1,
... fwhm=1,
... x0=0,
... position="x0",)
<Gaussian (Expression component)>
```

Substitutions for long or complicated expressions are separated by
semicolumns:

```pycon
>>> expr = 'A*B/(A+B) ; A = sin(x)+one; B = cos(y) - two; y = tan(x)'
>>> comp = hs.model.components1D.Expression(
...    expression=expr,
...    name='my function'
... )
>>> comp.parameters
(<Parameter one of my function component>,
 <Parameter two of my function component>)
```

documentation: Returns d(function)/d(parameter_1)

Bleasdale function component.

Also called the Bleasdale-Nelder function. Originates from
the description of the yield-density relationship in crop growth.

$$
f(x) = \left(a+b\cdot x\right)^{-1/c}
$$

# Parameters

a

: The value of Parameter a.

b

: The value of Parameter b.

c

: The value of Parameter c.

kwargs

: Extra keyword arguments are passed to
  {class}`~.api.model.components1D.Expression`.

# Notes

For $(a+b\cdot x)\leq0$, the component will be set to 0.

classes.name: hyperspy._components.logistic.Logistic
documentation:
Logistic function (sigmoid or s-shaped curve) component.

$$
f(x) = \frac{a}{1 + b\cdot \mathrm{exp}\left[-c
    \left((x - x_0\right)\right]}
$$

| Variable | Parameter |
| -------- | --------- |
| $A$      | a         |
| $b$      | b         |
| $c$      | c         |
| $x_0$    | origin    |

# Parameters

a

: The curve's maximum y-value,
  $\mathrm{lim}_{x\to\infty}\left(y\right) = a$

b

: Additional parameter:
  b>1 shifts origin to larger values;
  0\<b\<1 shifts origin to smaller values;
  b\<0 introduces an asymptote

c

: Logistic growth rate or steepness of the curve

origin

: Position of the sigmoid's midpoint

kwargs

: Extra keyword arguments are passed to the
  {class}`~.api.model.components1D.Expression` component.

classes.name: hyperspy._components.doniach.Doniach

methods.name: hyperspy._components.doniach.Doniach.estimate_parameters
documentation:
Estimate the Donach by calculating the median (centre) and the
variance parameter (sigma).

Note that an insufficient range will affect the accuracy of this
method and that this method doesn't estimate the asymmetry parameter
(alpha).

# Parameters

signal : {class}`~.api.signals.Signal1D`
x1 : float

> Defines the left limit of the spectral range to use for the
> estimation.

x2

: Defines the right limit of the spectral range to use for the
  estimation.

only_current

: If False estimates the parameters for the full dataset.

# Returns

bool

: Returns True when the parameters estimation is successful

# Examples

```pycon
>>> g = hs.model.components1D.Lorentzian()
>>> x = np.arange(-10, 10, 0.01)
>>> data = np.zeros((32, 32, 2000))
>>> data[:] = g.function(x).reshape((1, 1, 2000))
>>> s = hs.signals.Signal1D(data)
>>> s.axes_manager[-1].offset = -10
>>> s.axes_manager[-1].scale = 0.01
>>> g.estimate_parameters(s, -10, 10, False)
True
```

Doniach Sunjic lineshape component.

```{math}
:nowrap: true

\[
f(x) = \frac{A \cos[ \frac{{\pi\alpha}}{2}+
(1-\alpha)\tan^{-1}(\frac{x-centre+dx}{\sigma})]}
{(\sigma^2 + (x-centre+dx)^2)^{\frac{(1-\alpha)}{2}}}
\]


\[
dx = \frac{2.354820\sigma}{2 tan[\frac{\pi}{2-\alpha}]}
\]
```

| Variable | Parameter |
| -------- | --------- |
| $A$      | A         |
| $\sigma$ | sigma     |
| $\alpha$ | alpha     |
| $centre$ | centre    |

# Parameters

A

: Height

sigma

: Variance parameter of the distribution

alpha

: Tail or asymmetry parameter

centre

: Location of the maximum (peak position).

kwargs

: Extra keyword arguments are passed to the
  {class}`~.api.model.components1D.Expression` component.

# Notes

This is an asymmetric lineshape, originially design for xps but generally
useful for fitting peaks with low side tails
See Doniach S. and Sunjic M., J. Phys. 4C31, 285 (1970)
or <http://www.casaxps.com/help_manual/line_shapes.htm> for a more detailed
description

methods.name: hyperspy.signal.MVATools._plot_factors_or_pchars
documentation:
Plot components from PCA or ICA, or peak characteristics.

# Parameters

comp_ids

: If None, returns maps of all components.
  If int, returns maps of components with ids from 0 to given
  int.
  if list of ints, returns maps of components with ids in
  given list.

calibrate

: If True, plots are calibrated according to the data in the
  axes manager.

same_window

: If True, plots each factor to the same window. They are not scaled.
  Default True.

comp_label

: Title of the plot

cmap

: The colormap used for factor images or any peak characteristic
  scatter map overlay. Default is the matplotlib gray colormap
  (`plt.cm.gray`).

# Other Parameters

img_data

: The array to overlay peak characteristics onto. If None,
  defaults to the average image of your stack.

plot_shifts

: If true, plots a quiver (arrow) plot showing the shifts for
  each
  peak present in the component being plotted.

plot_char

: If int, the id of the characteristic to plot as the colored
  scatter plot.
  Possible components are:

  - 4: peak height
  - 5: peak orientation
  - 6: peak eccentricity

quiver_color

: Determines the color of vectors drawn for
  plotting peak shifts.

vector_scale

: Scales the quiver plot arrows. The vector is defined as one data
  unit along the X axis. If shifts are small, set vector_scale so
  that when they are multiplied by vector_scale, they are on the
  scale of the image plot. If None, uses matplotlib's autoscaling.

# Returns

matplotlib figure or list of figure if same_window=False
TODO: All of the plotting methods here should move to drawing

methods.name: hyperspy.signal.MVATools.plot_decomposition_factors
documentation:
Plot factors from a decomposition. In case of 1D signal axis, each
factors line can be toggled on and off by clicking on their
corresponding line in the legend.

# Parameters

comp_ids

: If `comp_ids` is `None`, maps of all components will be
  returned if the `output_dimension` was defined when executing
  {meth}`~hyperspy.api.signals.BaseSignal.decomposition`. Otherwise it
  raises a {exc}`ValueError`.
  If `comp_ids` is an int, maps of components with ids from 0 to
  the given value will be returned. If `comp_ids` is a list of
  ints, maps of components with ids contained in the list will be
  returned.

calibrate

: If `True`, calibrates plots where calibration is available
  from the axes_manager. If `False`, plots are in pixels/channels.

same_window

: If `True`, plots each factor to the same window. They are
  not scaled. Default is `True`.

title

: Title of the matplotlib plot or label of the line in the legend
  when the dimension of factors is 1 and `same_window` is `True`.

cmap

: The colormap used for the factor images, or for peak
  characteristics. Default is the matplotlib gray colormap
  (`plt.cm.gray`).

per_row

: The number of plots in each row, when the `same_window`
  parameter is `True`.

# See Also

plot_decomposition_loadings, plot_decomposition_results

comp_ids

: If `comp_ids` is `None`, maps of all components will be
  returned. If it is an int, maps of components with ids from 0 to
  the given value will be returned. If `comp_ids` is a list of
  ints, maps of components with ids contained in the list will be
  returned.

calibrate

: If `True`, calibrates plots where calibration is available
  from the axes_manager. If `False`, plots are in pixels/channels.

same_window

: if `True`, plots each factor to the same window. They are
  not scaled. Default is `True`.

title

: Title of the matplotlib plot or label of the line in the legend
  when the dimension of factors is 1 and `same_window` is `True`.

cmap

: The colormap used for the factor images, or for peak
  characteristics. Default is the matplotlib gray colormap
  (`plt.cm.gray`).

per_row

: The number of plots in each row, when the `same_window`
  parameter is `True`.

# See Also

plot_bss_loadings, plot_bss_results

methods.name: hyperspy.signal.MVATools.plot_decomposition_loadings
documentation:
Plot loadings from a decomposition. In case of 1D navigation axis,
each loading line can be toggled on and off by clicking on the legended
line.

# Parameters

comp_ids

: If `comp_ids` is `None`, maps of all components will be
  returned if the `output_dimension` was defined when executing
  {meth}`~hyperspy.api.signals.BaseSignal.decomposition`.
  Otherwise it raises a {exc}`ValueError`.
  If `comp_ids` is an int, maps of components with ids from 0 to
  the given value will be returned. If `comp_ids` is a list of
  ints, maps of components with ids contained in the list will be
  returned.

calibrate

: if `True`, calibrates plots where calibration is available
  from the axes_manager. If `False`, plots are in pixels/channels.

same_window

: if `True`, plots each factor to the same window. They are
  not scaled. Default is `True`.

title

: Title of the matplotlib plot or label of the line in the legend
  when the dimension of loadings is 1 and `same_window` is `True`.

with_factors

: If `True`, also returns figure(s) with the factors for the
  given comp_ids.

cmap

: The colormap used for the loadings images, or for peak
  characteristics. Default is the matplotlib gray colormap
  (`plt.cm.gray`).

no_nans

: If `True`, removes `NaN`'s from the loading plots.

per_row

: The number of plots in each row, when the `same_window`
  parameter is `True`.

axes_decor

: One of: `'all'`, `'ticks'`, `'off'`, or `None`
  Controls how the axes are displayed on each image; default is
  `'all'`
  If `'all'`, both ticks and axis labels will be shown.
  If `'ticks'`, no axis labels will be shown, but ticks/labels will.
  If `'off'`, all decorations and frame will be disabled.
  If `None`, no axis decorations will be shown, but ticks/frame
  will.

# See Also

plot_decomposition_factors, plot_decomposition_results

methods.name: hyperspy.signal.MVATools.export_decomposition_results
documentation:
Export results from a decomposition to any of the supported formats.

# Parameters

comp_ids

: If None, returns all components/loadings.
  If an int, returns components/loadings with ids from 0 to the
  given value.
  If a list of ints, returns components/loadings with ids provided in
  the given list.

folder

: The path to the folder where the file will be saved.
  If `None`, the current folder is used by default.

factor_prefix

: The prefix that any exported filenames for factors/components
  begin with

factor_format

: The extension of the format that you wish to save the factors to.
  Default is `'hspy'`. See `loading_format` for more details.

loading_prefix

: The prefix that any exported filenames for factors/components
  begin with

loading_format

: The extension of the format that you wish to save to. default
  is `'hspy'`. The format determines the kind of output:

  - For image formats (`'tif'`, `'png'`, `'jpg'`, etc.),
    plots are created using the plotting flags as below, and saved
    at 600 dpi. One plot is saved per loading.
  - For multidimensional formats (`'rpl'`, `'hspy'`), arrays
    are saved in single files. All loadings are contained in the
    one file.
  - For spectral formats (`'msa'`), each loading is saved to a
    separate file.

multiple_files

: If `True`, one file will be created for each factor and loading.
  Otherwise, only two files will be created, one for
  the factors and another for the loadings. The default value can
  be chosen in the preferences.

save_figures

: If `True` the same figures that are obtained when using the plot
  methods will be saved with 600 dpi resolution

# Notes

The following parameters are only used when `save_figures = True`

# Other Parameters

calibrate

: If `True`, calibrates plots where calibration is available
  from the axes_manager. If `False`, plots are in pixels/channels.

same_window

: If `True`, plots each factor to the same window.

comp_label

: the label that is either the plot title (if plotting in separate
  windows) or the label in the legend (if plotting in the same window)

cmap

: The colormap used for images, such as factors, loadings, or for peak
  characteristics. Default is the matplotlib gray colormap
  (`plt.cm.gray`).

per_row

: The number of plots in each row, when the `same_window`
  parameter is `True`.

save_figures_format

: The image format extension.

# See Also

get_decomposition_factors, get_decomposition_loadings

methods.name: hyperspy.signal.MVATools.plot_bss_results
documentation:
Plot the blind source separation factors and loadings.

Unlike {meth}`~hyperspy.api.signals.BaseSignal.plot_bss_factors` and
{meth}`~hyperspy.api.signals.BaseSignal.plot_bss_loadings`,
this method displays one component at a time. Therefore it provides a
more compact visualization than then other two methods.
The loadings and factors are displayed in different windows and each
has its own navigator/sliders to navigate them if they are
multidimensional. The component index axis is synchronized between
the two.

# Parameters

factors_navigator

: One of: `'smart_auto'`, `'auto'`, `None`, `'spectrum'` or a
  {class}`~hyperspy.api.signals.BaseSignal` object.
  `'smart_auto'` (default) displays sliders if the navigation
  dimension is less than 3. For a description of the other options
  see the {meth}`~hyperspy.api.signals.BaseSignal.plot` documentation
  for details.

loadings_navigator

: See the `factors_navigator` parameter

factors_dim

: Currently HyperSpy cannot plot a signal when the signal dimension is
  higher than two. Therefore, to visualize the BSS results when the
  factors or the loadings have signal dimension greater than 2,
  the data can be viewed as spectra (or images) by setting this
  parameter to 1 (or 2). (The default is 2)

loadings_dim

: See the `factors_dim` parameter

# See Also

plot_bss_factors, plot_bss_loadings, plot_decomposition_results

more compact visualization than then other two methods. The loadings

navigator/sliders to navigate them if they are multidimensional. The

# Parameters

  `'smart_auto'` (default) displays sliders if the navigation
  dimension is less than 3. For a description of the other options
  see the {meth}`~hyperspy.api.signals.BaseSignal.plot` documentation
  for details.

loadings_navigator

factors_dim, loadings_dim

: Currently HyperSpy cannot plot a signal when the signal dimension is
  higher than two. Therefore, to visualize the BSS results when the
  factors or the loadings have signal dimension greater than 2,
  the data can be viewed as spectra (or images) by setting this
  parameter to 1 (or 2). (The default is 2)

plot_decomposition_factors, plot_decomposition_loadings, plot_bss_results

methods.name: hyperspy.signal.MVATools.get_cluster_labels
documentation:
Return cluster labels as a Signal.

# Parameters

merged

: If False the cluster label signal has a navigation axes of length
  number_of_clusters and the signal along the the navigation
  direction is binary - 0 the point is not in the cluster, 1 it is
  included. If True, the cluster labels are merged (no navigation
  axes). The value of the signal at any point will be between -1 and
  the number of clusters. -1 represents the points that
  were masked for cluster analysis if any.

# See Also

get_cluster_signals

# Returns

{class}`~hyperspy.api.signals.BaseSignal`

: The cluster labels

methods.name: hyperspy.signal.BaseSignal.__init__
documentation:
Create a signal instance.

# Parameters

data

: The signal data. It can be an array of any dimensions.

axes

: List of either dictionaries or axes objects to define the axes (see
  the documentation of the {class}`~hyperspy.axes.AxesManager`
  class for more details).

attributes

: A dictionary whose items are stored as attributes.

metadata

: A dictionary containing a set of parameters
  that will to stores in the `metadata` attribute.
  Some parameters might be mandatory in some cases.

original_metadata

: A dictionary containing a set of parameters
  that will to stores in the `original_metadata` attribute. It
  typically contains all the parameters that has been
  imported from the original data file.

ragged

: Define whether the signal is ragged or not. Overwrite the
  `ragged` value in the `attributes` dictionary. If None, it does
  nothing. Default is None.

methods.name: hyperspy.signal.BaseSignal._deepcopy_with_new_data
documentation:
Returns a deepcopy of itself replacing the data.

This method has an advantage over the default {func}`copy.deepcopy`
in that it does not copy the data, which can save memory.

# Parameters

data : None or {class}`numpy.ndarray`
copy_variance : bool

> Whether to copy the variance of the signal to the new copy

copy_navigator

: Whether to copy the navigator of the signal to the new copy

copy_learning_results

: Whether to copy the learning_results of the signal to the new copy

# Returns

ns

: The newly copied signal

methods.name: hyperspy.signal.BaseSignal.as_lazy
documentation:
Create a copy of the given Signal as a
{class}`~hyperspy._signals.lazy.LazySignal`.

# Parameters

copy_variance

: Whether or not to copy the variance from the original Signal to
  the new lazy version. Default is True.

copy_navigator

: Whether or not to copy the navigator from the original Signal to
  the new lazy version. Default is True.

copy_learning_results

: Whether to copy the learning_results from the original signal to
  the new lazy version. Default is True.

res

: The same signal, converted to be lazy

Load data from dictionary.

file_data_dict

: A dictionary containing at least a 'data' keyword with an array of

  following items:

  - data: the signal data. It can be an array of any dimensions.
  - axes: a dictionary to define the axes (see the documentation of
    the {class}`~hyperspy.axes.AxesManager` class for more details).
  - attributes: a dictionary whose items are stored as attributes.

    mandatory in some cases.

    that will to stores in the `original_metadata` attribute. It
    typically contains all the parameters that has been
    imported from the original data file.

    Overwrite the attributes['ragged'] entry

methods.name: hyperspy.signal.BaseSignal.squeeze
documentation:
Remove single-dimensional entries from the shape of an array
and the axes. See {func}`numpy.squeeze` for more details.

# Returns

s

: A new signal object with single-entry dimensions removed

# Examples

```pycon
>>> s = hs.signals.Signal2D(np.random.random((2, 1, 1, 6, 8, 8)))
>>> s
<Signal2D, title: , dimensions: (6, 1, 1, 2|8, 8)>
>>> s = s.squeeze()
>>> s
<Signal2D, title: , dimensions: (6, 2|8, 8)>
```

methods.name: hyperspy.signal.BaseSignal._to_dictionary
documentation:
Returns a dictionary that can be used to recreate the signal.

All items but `data` are copies.

Parameters
----------
add_learning_results : bool, optional
    Whether or not to include any multivariate learning results in
    the outputted dictionary. Default is True.
add_models : bool, optional
    Whether or not to include any models in the outputted dictionary.
    Default is False
add_original_metadata : bool
    Whether or not to include the original_medata in the outputted
    dictionary. Default is True.

Returns
-------
dic : dict
    The dictionary that can be used to recreate the signal
